prompt: Which work in the corpus illustrates the doctrine that virtues and vices arise from association by telling of two parrots named Gāvākṣa and Girikā — one raised by a hunter and the other by a scholar — where a king who has lost his retinue is first brought to the hunter's house and the parrot Girikā cries out 'Come on, come on! Smash his head!' while the scholar's parrot Gāvākṣa instead welcomes the king with words of greeting, offers him a new mat to sit on, betel nut and fresh betel leaf with uncooked lime to chew, and invites him to bathe in the spring? Quote the parrots' two contrasting speeches verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The named pair of parrots Gāvākṣa and Girikā, the explicit hunter-vs-scholar upbringing contrast, the king-separated-from-retinue frame, and the two specific quoted speeches (the violent 'Smash his head!' from Girikā vs Gāvākṣa's hospitable betel-nut-and-spring-bath welcome) form a single named exemplum that is unique to this Old Javanese verse compendium's bilingual gloss; no other corpus work — neither the Sanskrit smṛtis, the Vīramitrodaya digest, the Old English religious poems, the Urdu dīwāns, the French treatises, nor any other Old Javanese text in the corpus — contains this two-parrots-and-the-lost-king narrative with these proper names.

prompt: Which work in the corpus argues that nothing is without a flaw by listing a catalogue that includes the lotus being full of thorns, the snowy mountain having its cold, the sandalwood tree being inhabited by serpents in its hollows, the moon's body stained by a hare, the ocean's salty water — and then turns to the gods themselves: that Viṣṇu was a cowherd, that Indra is exceedingly fickle, and that Śaṅkara has a blue throat from the kālakūṭa poison — concluding that if even the gods carry such defects, how much more so will people on earth? Quote the verse listing these specific flaws verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The single ślokā paired with its bilingual gloss enumerating exactly these flaws — lotus/thorns, snowy mountain/cold, sandalwood/serpents, moon/hare-stain, ocean/salt, sun/heat, Viṣṇu-as-cowherd, Indra-as-fickle, Śaṅkara/blue-throat-from-kālakūṭa — and using them to argue an a-fortiori case about human imperfection is unique to this work. The Sanskrit smṛtis and the Vīramitrodaya digest do not assemble this catalogue of divine flaws; the Old Javanese Saiva, Buddhist and tantric texts in the corpus (Vrhaspatitattva, Ganapatitattva, San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna, Kuñjarakarṇa-dharmakathana, Kalpabuddha) do not contain this specific divine-flaws inventory paired with the natural-world flaws.

prompt: Which work in the corpus illustrates the maxim that even a stranger can be a true kinsman and a kinsman can be an enemy — pairing the abstract claim that a disease born of the body is harmful while a forest herb that helps is a friend with a specifically named exemplum: that Sugrīva was pained by his own kinsman Bāli, yet sought out Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa, though strangers, in the middle of the forest, to serve as the medicine for Sugrīva's pain? Quote the verse and the named exemplum verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The Rāmāyaṇa-derived foursome (Sugrīva, Bāli, Rāma, Lakṣmaṇa) is appropriated here not as narrative but as a moral exemplum embedded in a bilingual ślokā-and-gloss compendium, with the specific medical metaphor (kinsman-as-disease, stranger-as-forest-herb-medicine) attached to the named characters and the specific 'middle of the forest' setting for the seeking. No other corpus work — the Sanskrit smṛtis and the Vīramitrodaya digest, which do not narrate Rāmāyaṇa episodes; the Old Javanese Buddhist and Saiva texts, which when they reference Rāmāyaṇa figures do not pair them with this kinsman-as-disease framing; nor the Old English religious poems, French treatises, or Urdu dīwāns — contains this exact pairing of the four names with the forest-herb-as-friend image.
